content:
Hello memory agent, you are continuing to observe the primary Claude session.

<observed_from_primary_session>
  <user_request>[ASK:commit3] yes</user_request>
  <requested_at>2026-07-11</requested_at>
</observed_from_primary_session>

You are a Claude-Mem, a specialized observer tool for creating searchable memory FOR FUTURE SESSIONS.

CRITICAL: Record what was LEARNED/BUILT/FIXED/DEPLOYED/CONFIGURED, not what you (the observer) are doing.

You do not have access to tools. All information you need is provided in <observed_from_primary_session> messages. Create observations from what you observe - no investigation needed.

Your job is to monitor a different Claude Code session happening RIGHT NOW, with the goal of creating observations and progress summaries as the work is being done LIVE by the user. You are NOT the one doing the work - you are ONLY observing and recording what is being built, fixed, deployed, or configured in the other session.

SPATIAL AWARENESS: Tool executions include the working directory (tool_cwd) to help you understand:
- Which repository/project is being worked on
- Where files are located relative to the project root
- How to match requested paths to actual execution paths

WHAT TO RECORD
--------------
Focus on deliverables and capabilities:
- What the system NOW DOES differently (new capabilities)
- What shipped to users/production (features, fixes, configs, docs)
- Changes in technical domains (auth, data, UI, infra, DevOps, docs)

Use verbs like: implemented, fixed, deployed, configured, migrated, optimized, added, refactored

✅ GOOD EXAMPLES (describes what was built):
- "Authentication now supports OAuth2 with PKCE flow"
- "Deployment pipeline runs canary releases with auto-rollback"
- "Database indexes optimized for common query patterns"

❌ BAD EXAMPLES (describes observation process - DO NOT DO THIS):
- "Analyzed authentication implementation and stored findings"
- "Tracked deployment steps and logged outcomes"
- "Monitored database performance and recorded metrics"

WHEN TO SKIP
------------
Skip routine operations:
- Empty status checks
- Package installations with no errors
- Simple file listings
- Repetitive operations you've already documented
- If file related research comes back as empty or not found
- **No output necessary if skipping.**

IMPORTANT: Continue generating observations from tool use messages using the XML structure below.

OUTPUT FORMAT
-------------
Output observations using this XML structure:

```xml
<observation>
  <type>[ bugfix | feature | refactor | change | discovery | decision ]</type>
  <!--
    **type**: MUST be EXACTLY one of these 6 options (no other values allowed):
      - bugfix: something was broken, now fixed
      - feature: new capability or functionality added
      - refactor: code restructured, behavior unchanged
      - change: generic modification (docs, config, misc)
      - discovery: learning about existing system
      - decision: architectural/design choice with rationale
  -->
  <title>[**title**: Short title capturing the core action or topic]</title>
  <subtitle>[**subtitle**: One sentence explanation (max 24 words)]</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
  </facts>
  <!--
    **facts**: Concise, self-contained statements
Each fact is ONE piece of information
      No pronouns - each fact must stand alone
      Include specific details: filenames, functions, values

**files**: All files touched (full paths from project root)
  -->
  <narrative>[**narrative**: Full context: What was done, how it works, why it matters]</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
  </concepts>
  <!--
    **concepts**: 2-5 knowledge-type categories. MUST use ONLY these exact keywords:
      - how-it-works: understanding mechanisms
      - why-it-exists: purpose or rationale
      - what-changed: modifications made
      - problem-solution: issues and their fixes
      - gotcha: traps or edge cases
      - pattern: reusable approach
      - trade-off: pros/cons of a decision

    IMPORTANT: Do NOT include the observation type (change/discovery/decision) as a concept.
    Types and concepts are separate dimensions.
  -->
  <files_read>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>
```


IMPORTANT! DO NOT do any work right now other than generating this OBSERVATIONS from tool use messages - and remember that you are a memory agent designed to summarize a DIFFERENT claude code session, not this one.

Never reference yourself or your own actions. Do not output anything other than the observation content formatted in the XML structure above. All other output is ignored by the system, and the system has been designed to be smart about token usage. Please spend your tokens wisely on useful observations.

Remember that we record these observations as a way of helping us stay on track with our progress, and to help us keep important decisions and changes at the forefront of our minds! :) Thank you so much for your help!

MEMORY PROCESSING CONTINUED
===========================

PROGRESS SUMMARY CHECKPOINT
===========================
Write progress notes of what was done, what was learned, and what's next. This is a checkpoint to capture progress so far. The session is ongoing - you may receive more requests and tool executions after this summary. Write "next_steps" as the current trajectory of work (what's actively being worked on or coming up next), not as post-session future work. Always write at least a minimal summary explaining current progress, even if work is still in early stages, so that users see a summary output tied to each request.

Claude's Full Response to User:
## 三項全部處理完成

**Item 1（zombie skills）**：依你的決定保留。三個 skill 的 store entry 已重建並標 `pinned=true`（skilllint 規則 5 會跳過 underused 檢查，不再吵），正本與 junction 都不動。

**Item 2（uk-conventions 誤報）**：skilllint prompt 已加 notes 排除規則——entry notes 已標明 false positive 的跳過不報。這是 durable 修法，因為 `markUsed` 更新時會保留 notes。

**Item 3（fact hit 恆 0）**：根因是 fact embedding 從未被計算（195 筆 facts 與 embedding_cache join 重疊 = 0），已加 `backfillFactEmbeddings()` 啟動補算 + `insertFact` 即時補嵌入，tsc 通過。

STATE.md 三項都已標記處理結果，教訓也存進記憶了。

## 待確認：commit

bridge repo 目前有 3 個本次修改的檔案：

- `src/memory-db.ts`（+24：backfill 函式 + insertFact 補嵌入）
- `src/index.ts`（+6：啟動非阻塞補算）
- `src/commands/memory.ts`（skilllint prompt 一行）

另有 `docs/usage-guide.html` 是 session 開始前就存在的修改（上次 dream 的 docupdate），**不會**包進這次 commit。

擬定 commit message：`fix(memory): 補 fact embedding backfill 修復 fact recall 恆零 + skilllint notes 排除規則`

注意：commit 後需要 build + 重啟 bridge 才生效（重啟後首次啟動會補算 195 筆嵌入，之後 hit-log 應開始出現 fact 記錄）。

<<ASK:commit3|*yes=Commit（3檔）|edit_msg=改 message|no=先不 commit>>

Respond in this XML format:
<summary>
  <request>[Short title capturing the user's request AND the substance of what was discussed/done]</request>
  <investigated>[What has been explored so far? What was examined?]</investigated>
  <learned>[What have you learned about how things work?]</learned>
  <completed>[What work has been completed so far? What has shipped or changed?]</completed>
  <next_steps>[What are you actively working on or planning to work on next in this session?]</next_steps>
  <notes>[Additional insights or observations about the current progress]</notes>
</summary>

IMPORTANT! DO NOT do any work right now other than generating this next PROGRESS SUMMARY - and remember that you are a memory agent designed to summarize a DIFFERENT claude code session, not this one.

Never reference yourself or your own actions. Do not output anything other than the summary content formatted in the XML structure above. All other output is ignored by the system, and the system has been designed to be smart about token usage. Please spend your tokens wisely on useful summary content.

Thank you, this summary will be very useful for keeping track of our progress!

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.thinking:
The user is asking me to generate a progress summary for a memory agent observing another Claude Code session. Let me analyze what happened:

From the observed session:
- The user responded "yes" to a commit prompt (ASK:commit3)
- Claude's full response shows they completed three items:
  1. **Zombie skills**: Rebuilt store entries with `pinned=true` to bypass skilllint's underused checks
  2. **uk-conventions false positive**: Added notes to skilllint prompt to skip false positives marked in entry notes
  3. **Fact hit count always 0**: Root cause was fact embeddings were never computed (195 facts had 0 overlap with embedding_cache). Fixed by adding `backfillFactEmbeddings()` function and making `insertFact` compute embeddings immediately.

- Files modified in bridge repo:
  - `src/memory-db.ts` (+24 lines: backfill function + insertFact embedding fix)
  - `src/index.ts` (+6 lines: startup non-blocking backfill)
  - `src/commands/memory.ts` (skilllint prompt update)
  - `docs/usage-guide.html` (pre-existing change from previous session, not included in this commit)

- Proposed commit message about fixing fact embedding backfill
- User confirmed "yes" to commit

The session appears to be fixing memory system issues, specifically around fact embeddings and skill store management.

Let me write a summary from the observer's perspective, focusing on what was DONE/BUILT/FIXED, not what I'm observing.

content.text:
<summary>
  <request>Commit memory system fixes for fact embeddings and skill store management</request>
  
  <investigated>Three memory system issues were investigated: zombie skills triggering underused warnings, uk-conventions skill false positive in skilllint, and fact recall returning zero hits despite 195 stored facts. Root cause analysis revealed fact embeddings were never computed (zero overlap between facts table and embedding_cache).</investigated>
  
  <learned>Fact embeddings require explicit computation during insertion and weren't being generated for existing facts. The `insertFact` function in memory-db.ts was missing embedding generation logic. Skilllint's underused skill detection (rule 5) can be bypassed by setting `pinned=true` in skill store entries. Entry notes can mark false positives that skilllint should skip.</learned>
  
  <completed>Three fixes implemented in bridge repo: (1) Zombie skills marked as pinned in store to bypass underused checks, (2) Skilllint prompt updated to skip entries with false-positive notes, (3) Fact embedding system fixed with backfillFactEmbeddings() function added to memory-db.ts and insertFact modified to compute embeddings immediately. Changes span src/memory-db.ts (+24 lines), src/index.ts (+6 lines for startup backfill), and src/commands/memory.ts (skilllint prompt). User confirmed commit of these three files.</completed>
  
  <next_steps>Commit the three modified files with message about fact embedding backfill fix. After commit, build and restart bridge service to trigger backfill of 195 existing fact embeddings. Monitor hit-log after restart to verify fact recall now returns non-zero results.</next_steps>
  
  <notes>The backfill runs non-blocking on startup to avoid delaying service initialization. Pre-existing docs/usage-guide.html changes from previous session are excluded from this commit. All three STATE.md items now resolved with fixes and lessons stored in memory.</notes>
</summary>

content.thinking:
The user committed the memory system fixes. Let me analyze what was done:

1. **Git commit** of three files:
   - src/memory-db.ts
   - src/index.ts
   - src/commands/memory.ts

2. **Commit message** (in Chinese with detailed technical explanation):
   - Fixed fact embedding backfill to repair fact recall always returning zero
   - Added skilllint notes exclusion rule
   - Detailed breakdown of changes in each file

3. **Root cause** described in commit:
   - Only queries were being embedded, not facts
   - 195 facts had zero overlap with embedding_cache
   - vectorSearch returned empty, hybrid scores maxed at 0.3×decay below 0.2 threshold
   - Result: hit-log fact count always 0

4. **Changes made**:
   - memory-db.ts: Added backfillFactEmbeddings() (idempotent gap-fill); insertFact now fire-and-forget embeds
   - index.ts: After syncFactsToDb, non-blocking backfill of historical fact embeddings
   - commands/memory.ts: skilllint prompt orphan cleanup added notes exclusion rule
   - Note about uk-conventions being custom command not skill, so deleting entry isn't durable

5. **Outcome**: 
   - Commit SHA: 14d81ad
   - 3 files changed, 30 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)

This is a significant bugfix - the fact embedding system was broken and now it's fixed. This should be recorded as a bugfix observation.

Type: bugfix (fact recall was broken, now fixed)
Title: Fixed fact embedding generation to enable fact recall
Subtitle: Fact embeddings were never computed causing zero recall hits; added backfill and immediate embedding on insert
Facts:
The core issue was that 195 facts never got embedded, leaving vectorSearch with no candidates and hybrid scoring stuck below threshold. The fix adds idempotent backfilling to memory-db.ts and triggers non-blocking embedding on startup, while insertFact now immediately embeds new facts. The skilllint command also got updated to exclude false-positive notes entries. The core issue was that embeddings require explicit computation—they don't generate automatically. I implemented a three-file solution: backfillFactEmbeddings() in memory-db.ts handles historical data, insertFact now triggers embedding generation immediately, and index.ts initiates the non-blocking backfill on startup. The memory.ts command file was also updated as part of this fix.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>bugfix</type>
  <title>Fixed fact embedding generation to enable fact recall</title>
  <subtitle>Fact embeddings were never computed causing zero recall hits; added backfill and immediate embedding on insert</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>195 stored facts had zero overlap with embedding_cache table preventing any fact matches</fact>
    <fact>vectorSearch returned empty results causing hybrid scores to stay below 0.2 threshold</fact>
    <fact>backfillFactEmbeddings() added to src/memory-db.ts for idempotent gap-filling of historical facts</fact>
    <fact>insertFact function modified to fire-and-forget embed new facts immediately upon insertion</fact>
    <fact>src/index.ts triggers non-blocking backfill on startup after syncFactsToDb completes</fact>
    <fact>skilllint prompt updated in src/commands/memory.ts to skip entries with false-positive notes</fact>
    <fact>Commit 14d81ad includes 30 insertions and 2 deletions across 3 files</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>The memory system's fact recall was completely broken - despite 195 facts stored in the database, hit-log consistently showed zero fact matches. Root cause analysis revealed fact embeddings were never being computed; only query embeddings existed in embedding_cache. The facts table and embedding_cache had zero join overlap, causing vectorSearch to return empty and hybrid scores to max at 0.3×decay (below the 0.2 relevance threshold). Fixed with three-part solution: (1) added backfillFactEmbeddings() function to memory-db.ts for one-time historical backfill (idempotent, only computes missing embeddings), (2) modified insertFact to compute embeddings immediately via fire-and-forget pattern, and (3) triggered non-blocking backfill in index.ts on startup after database sync. Additionally updated skilllint prompt to respect notes field for false-positive exclusions, addressing uk-conventions custom command misclassification issue. After bridge restart, 195 historical fact embeddings will be computed and future facts will embed on insertion, enabling fact recall to function properly.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>gotcha</concept>
    <concept>what-changed</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>src/memory-db.ts</file>
    <file>src/index.ts</file>
    <file>src/commands/memory.ts</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>
